items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Syndromes: Endocrinologic/Hematologic | ENDOCRINE PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES
items.title: Ectopic Hormone Production in Paraneoplastic Syndromes
items.content: Neoplastic cells can produce hormones ectopically, leading to paraneoplastic syndromes. Ectopic hormone production involves abnormal regulation and peptide processing, often resulting from cellular dedifferentiation or rare genetic rearrangements.
keyPoints: Ectopic expression refers to hormone production by atypical tissue sources.
Common ectopic hormones include ACTH, PTHrP, hCG, $\alpha$-fetoprotein.
Cellular dedifferentiation is a major cause of ectopic hormone production.

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Syndromes: Endocrinologic/Hematologic | ENDOCRINE PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES
items.title: Clinical Implications of Ectopic Hormone Production
items.content: Ectopic hormone production can cause significant morbidity and may present as the initial clinical feature of an underlying malignancy. Diagnosis involves confirming excess hormone production from atypical sources, often using immunostaining or imaging studies.
keyPoints: Excessive hormones like ACTH, PTHrP, and vasopressin lead to clinical syndromes.
Imaging (CT, MRI, PET) aids in tumor detection when endocrine symptoms precede cancer diagnosis.
Treatment focuses on the underlying tumor; specific therapies may alleviate symptoms.

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Syndromes: Endocrinologic/Hematologic | ENDOCRINE PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES
items.title: Common Paraneoplastic Endocrine Syndromes
items.content: Certain paraneoplastic syndromes frequently occur due to hormone overproduction, such as hypercalcemia from PTHrP, hyponatremia from vasopressin, and Cushing's syndrome from ectopic ACTH.
keyPoints: Hypercalcemia is linked with PTHrP overproduction.
Hyponatremia results from excess vasopressin.
Cushing's syndrome arises from ectopic ACTH production.

items.title: Understanding Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy (HHM) and its Etiology
items.content: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy (HHM) is a paraneoplastic syndrome occurring in up to 20% of cancer patients, most commonly associated with lung, head and neck, skin, esophagus, breast, genitourinary tract cancers, multiple myeloma, lymphomas, and their metastases. The primary cause is the overproduction of PTHrP, which mimics parathyroid hormone (PTH) by binding to its receptor, leading to similar biochemical features as hyperparathyroidism.
keyPoints: HHM occurs in up to 20% of cancer patients.
Most common in cancers of the lung, head and neck, skin, esophagus, breast, genitourinary tract, multiple myeloma, lymphomas, and metastases.
Caused primarily by overproduction of PTHrP.
PTHrP binds to the PTH receptor, mimicking hyperparathyroidism.
Elevated PTHrP levels are found in ~80% of hypercalcemic cancer patients.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · (See also Chap. 422).

items.subtopic: (See also Chap. 422).
items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Features of Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy (HHM)
items.content: Patients with HHM often present with hypercalcemia, which may be discovered during routine tests or as an initial symptom of malignancy. Marked hypercalcemia (>3.5 mmol/L [>14 mg/dL]) can lead to symptoms such as fatigue, mental status changes, polyuria, dehydration, and nephrolithiasis. Diagnosis is supported by the presence of known malignancy, recent onset of hypercalcemia, very high serum calcium levels, metabolic alkalosis, and suppressed PTH levels.
keyPoints: Hypercalcemia may be an initial or discovered symptom in HHM patients.
Marked hypercalcemia (>3.5 mmol/L [>14 mg/dL]) can cause significant symptoms.
HHM diagnosis is supported by known malignancy, recent onset of hypercalcemia, very high serum calcium levels, metabolic alkalosis, and suppressed PTH levels.
PTHrP level measurement confirms HHM in ~80% of cases.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · (See also Chap. 422).

items.subtopic: Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy
items.title: Management Strategies for Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy (HHM)
items.content: The management of HHM involves dietary calcium restriction, medications, and intravenous solutions. Initial treatment includes saline rehydration to dilute serum calcium and promote calciuresis, with caution in patients with cardiac, hepatic, or renal insufficiency. Loop diuretics like furosemide can enhance calcium excretion but are secondary to hydration and fluid balance monitoring. Oral phosphorus is administered until serum phosphorus exceeds 1 mmol/L. Bisphosphonates (e.g., zoledronate, pamidronate) reduce serum calcium within 1-2 days and suppress release for weeks. Denosumab can be used if bisphosphonates are ineffective. Cinacalcet is applicable in parathyroid carcinoma and ectopic PTH-producing tumors. Glucocorticoids may treat hypercalcemia associated with lymphomas, multiple myeloma, or leukemia. Dialysis is considered for severe cases unresponsive to other treatments.
keyPoints: Saline rehydration typically 200–500 mL/h
Loop diuretics (furosemide 20–80 mg IV) after rehydration
Oral phosphorus until serum phosphorus >1 mmol/L (>3 mg/dL)
Bisphosphonates: zoledronate (4–8 mg IV), pamidronate (60–90 mg IV), etidronate (7.5 mg/kg PO for 3–7 days)
Denosumab (120 mg SC weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly) if bisphosphonates ineffective
Cinacalcet (30 mg PO bid to 90 mg qid) for parathyroid carcinoma and ectopic PTH tumors
Glucocorticoids (prednisone 40–100 mg PO in four divided doses) for lymphomas, multiple myeloma, or leukemia
Dialysis considered for severe hypercalcemia unresponsive to other treatments

items.subtopic: ECTOPIC VASOPRESSIN: TUMOR-ASSOCIATED SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE
items.title: Etiology and Clinical Manifestations of Ectopic Vasopressin Production in SIADH
items.content: Ectopic vasopressin production, commonly by tumors like SCLC, is a frequent cause of SIADH. Nonneoplastic causes include CNS trauma, infections, and medications. Compensatory mechanisms may delay hyponatremia development, but prolonged excessive vasopressin can reset the osmostat controlling thirst and secretion. Hyponatremia develops slowly but can worsen with IV fluids or new medications. Symptoms range from weakness to seizures, reflecting onset rapidity and severity.
keyPoints: Ectopic vasopressin production is common in SCLC
SIADH also caused by CNS trauma, infections, medications
Compensatory responses may delay hyponatremia
Hyponatremia develops slowly but can worsen with IV fluids or new meds
Symptoms: weakness, lethargy, nausea, confusion, seizures
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy | ECTOPIC VASOPRESSIN: TUMOR-ASSOCIATED SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE

items.subtopic: ECTOPIC VASOPRESSIN: TUMOR-ASSOCIATED SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE
items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Ectopic Vasopressin Production in SIADH
items.content: Diagnosis of ectopic vasopressin production involves identifying hyponatremia and reduced serum osmolality with inappropriately normal or increased urine osmolality. Urine sodium excretion is typically normal unless volume depletion exists. Exclude other causes like renal, adrenal, or thyroid insufficiency, and consider physiologic sources of vasopressin stimulation (CNS lesions, pulmonary disease) or medications. Vasopressin measurements are generally unnecessary for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Hyponatremia with reduced serum osmolality
Inappropriately normal/increased urine osmolality
Urine sodium excretion normal unless volume depleted
Exclude renal, adrenal, thyroid insufficiency
Consider physiologic sources of vasopressin stimulation and medications

items.subtopic: Ectopic Vasopressin: Tumor-Associated SIADH
items.title: Management of Hyponatremia in Ectopic Vasopressin Production
items.content: Hyponatremia due to ectopic vasopressin production should be corrected gradually unless there are severe symptoms like altered mental status or risk of seizures. Fluid restriction is often effective, but requires strict monitoring. In cases of severe hyponatremia (Na <115 meq/L) or significant mental status changes, hypertonic saline with furosemide may be necessary.
keyPoints: Hyponatremia develops over weeks to months in ectopic vasopressin production.
Correction should be gradual unless there is altered mental status or seizure risk.
Rapid correction can cause brain dehydration and central pontine myelinolysis.
Fluid restriction often suffices, but requires strict monitoring.
Severe hyponatremia (Na <115 meq/L) may require hypertonic saline with furosemide.
Sodium correction rate should be 0.5–1 meq/L per hour to prevent central pontine myelinolysis.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Ectopic Vasopressin: Tumor-Associated SIADH

items.subtopic: Ectopic Vasopressin: Tumor-Associated SIADH
items.title: Pharmacologic Interventions for Ectopic Vasopressin-Induced Hyponatremia
items.content: Demeclocycline can inhibit vasopressin action but has a slow onset (1–2 weeks). Vaptan drugs like tolvaptan and conivaptan are more effective, especially when combined with fluid restriction. Tolvaptan is administered at 15 mg PO daily, while conivaptan ranges from 20–120 mg PO bid or 10–40 mg IV.
keyPoints: Demeclocycline (150–300 mg orally 3–4 times daily) has a slow onset of action (1–2 weeks).
Vaptans inhibit vasopressin receptors ($ V_{1A} $, $ V_{1B} $, $ V_{2} $) in renal collecting ducts.
Tolvaptan is given at 15 mg PO daily; conivaptan ranges from 20–120 mg PO bid or 10–40 mg IV.
Vaptans are effective with fluid restriction for euvolemic hyponatremia.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Ectopic Vasopressin: Tumor-Associated SIADH

items.subtopic: CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).
items.title: Etiology and Common Causes of Ectopic ACTH Production in Cushing's Syndrome
items.content: Ectopic ACTH production accounts for 10–20% of cases of Cushing's syndrome, primarily due to neuroendocrine tumors. The most common cause is small cell lung cancer (SCLC), followed by bronchial and thymic carcinoids, islet cell tumors, other carcinoids, and pheochromocytomas.
keyPoints: Ectopic ACTH production accounts for 10–20% of Cushing's syndrome cases.
Common causes include SCLC, bronchial/thymic carcinoids, islet cell tumors, other carcinoids, and pheochromocytomas.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).

items.subtopic: CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Ectopic ACTH Syndrome
items.content: Diagnosis of ectopic ACTH syndrome is facilitated by elevated urine-free cortisol levels (>2-4 times normal) and plasma ACTH levels usually >22 pmol/L (>100 pg/mL). High-dose dexamethasone suppression tests are typically ineffective in suppressing serum cortisol in ectopic ACTH cases, unlike pituitary adenomas.
keyPoints: Urine-free cortisol levels typically greater than two to four times normal.
Plasma ACTH level usually >22 pmol/L (>100 pg/mL).
High-dose dexamethasone suppression fails in ~90% of ectopic ACTH cases.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).

items.subtopic: CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).
items.title: Clinical Manifestations Distinguishing Ectopic ACTH Syndrome
items.content: Ectopic ACTH syndrome is characterized by less marked weight gain and centripetal fat redistribution, fluid retention, hypertension, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, glucose intolerance, and increased pigmentation. High cortisol levels can lead to skin fragility and severe hypokalemia due to mineralocorticoid receptor activity.
keyPoints: Less marked weight gain and centripetal fat redistribution compared to other Cushing's causes.
Associated with fluid retention, hypertension, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, glucose intolerance.
Increased pigmentation from MSH derived from POMC precursor peptide.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).

items.subtopic: Cushing's Syndrome Caused by Ectopic ACTH Production
items.title: Management of Hypercortisolism in Ectopic ACTH Syndrome
items.content: Patients with ectopic ACTH production often experience significant morbidity due to extreme cortisol excess, leading to metabolic derangements and increased risk of infections. Medical therapy is typically the most practical approach for managing hypercortisolism, as surgical options like adrenalectomy are not always feasible.
keyPoints: Ectopic ACTH syndrome can cause depression, personality changes, diabetes mellitus, hypokalemia, poor wound healing, and increased risk of infections.
Patients have an elevated risk of venous thromboembolism due to malignancy and altered coagulation profiles.

Medical therapies include ketoconazole (300–600 mg PO bid), metyrapone (250–500 mg PO every 6 h), mitotane (3–6 g PO in four divided doses, tapered), etomidate (0.1–0.3 mg/kg/h IV), and mifepristone (200–1000 mg PO qd).
Glucocorticoid replacement is necessary to prevent adrenal insufficiency.
ACTH-neutralizing antibodies and ACTH receptor blockers are under investigation.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Cushing's Syndrome Caused by Ectopic ACTH Production | TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II

items.subtopic: TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II
items.title: Diagnosis of Tumor-Induced Hypoglycemia via IGF-II Pathway
items.content: Mesenchymal tumors and other large tumors can produce excessive IGF-II, leading to hypoglycemia. Diagnosis involves documenting low serum glucose with suppressed insulin levels during symptomatic episodes, with an elevated IGF-II/IGF-I ratio greater than 10:1 being suggestive.
keyPoints: Tumors producing excess IGF-II can lead to hypoglycemia by suppressing GH and insulin, reducing IGFBP-3 and ALS.
Hypoglycemia is often associated with fasting and tumors larger than 10 cm in size.
Symptoms include sweating, tremors, palpitations, confusion, seizures, or coma.
Diagnosis: low serum glucose, suppressed insulin levels, and an IGF-II/IGF-I ratio >10:1.
Increased IGF-II mRNA expression is common in these tumors.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Cushing's Syndrome Caused by Ectopic ACTH Production | TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II

items.subtopic: TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II
items.title: Treatment Strategies for Tumor-Induced Hypoglycemia
items.content: Management of tumor-induced hypoglycemia involves treating the underlying malignancy if possible, frequent meals, IV glucose during fasting, and medications like glucagon, recombinant GH, and glucocorticoids to enhance glucose production. Antibodies inhibiting IGF-II action are under development.
keyPoints: Treatment of the underlying tumor may reduce hypoglycemia risk.
Frequent meals and IV glucose are necessary during fasting or sleep.
Glucagon, recombinant GH, and glucocorticoids can be used to enhance glucose production.
Antibodies inhibiting IGF-II action are being developed.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Cushing's Syndrome Caused by Ectopic ACTH Production | TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II

items.subtopic: HUMAN CHORIONIC GONADOTROPIN | ONCOGENIC OSTEOMALACIA
items.title: Ectopic hCG Production and its Clinical Implications
items.content: Ectopic production of intact human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) is often associated with various malignancies, including testicular embryonal tumors, germ cell tumors, extragonadal germinomas, lung cancer, hepatoma, and pancreatic islet tumors. In men, elevated hCG levels can lead to increased estrogen production due to stimulated steroidogenesis and aromatase activity in testicular Leydig cells, resulting in gynecomastia or precocious puberty.
keyPoints: Ectopic hCG production commonly occurs with lung cancer and pancreatic islet tumors.
High hCG levels can cause gynecomastia in men by increasing estrogen production.
Precocious puberty or gynecomastia should prompt measurement of hCG to consider a testicular tumor or ectopic source.
Treatment targets the underlying malignancy.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · HUMAN CHORIONIC GONADOTROPIN | ONCOGENIC OSTEOMALACIA

items.subtopic: HUMAN CHORIONIC GONADOTROPIN | ONCOGENIC OSTEOMALACIA
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Oncogenic Osteomalacia
items.content: Oncogenic osteomalacia, characterized by hypophosphatemia and renal phosphate wasting due to excessive production of fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23), leads to muscle weakness, bone pain, and osteomalacia. It is often caused by benign mesenchymal tumors such as hemangiopericytomas, fibromas, and giant cell tumors. Diagnosis involves measuring serum phosphorus levels and FGF23, with tumor resection confirming the diagnosis.
keyPoints: Oncogenic osteomalacia presents with reduced serum phosphorus and renal phosphate wasting.
Commonly caused by benign mesenchymal tumors like hemangiopericytomas and fibromas.
Resection of the causative tumor reverses symptoms, confirming diagnosis.
Treatment includes tumor removal, phosphate supplementation, vitamin D, and potentially octreotide or burosumab.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · HUMAN CHORIONIC GONADOTROPIN | ONCOGENIC OSTEOMALACIA

items.subtopic: CONSUMPTIVE HYPOTHYROIDISM
items.title: Mechanism and Management of Consumptive Hypothyroidism in Newborns with Hepatic Hemangiomas
items.content: Consumptive hypothyroidism in newborns is caused by overexpression of type 3 deiodinase (D3) due to hepatic hemangiomas, leading to degradation of thyroid hormones. This results in low T4 and T3 levels, high TSH, and elevated reverse T3. Management includes treating the underlying hemangioma and administering L-thyroxine replacement, with potential benefits from steroids and propranolol.
keyPoints: Overexpression of type 3 deiodinase (D3) leads to degradation of thyroid hormones.
Characterized by low T4, low T3, high TSH, and elevated reverse T3 (rT3).
Treatment involves L-thyroxine replacement titrated to normalize TSH.
Steroids and propranolol may inhibit growth factor pathways stimulating D3 production.

items.subtopic: CANCER IMMUNOTHERAPY-ASSOCIATED ENDOCRINOPATHIES
items.title: Autoimmune Endocrine Diseases Associated with Cancer Immunotherapy
items.content: Cancer immunotherapies, especially immune checkpoint inhibitors like anti-CTLA-4 and PD-1/PD-L1, are linked to a ~10% incidence of autoimmune endocrine diseases. Common conditions include hypophysitis and thyroid disorders, with potential involvement of adrenal insufficiency, hypoparathyroidism, and diabetes insipidus. These effects may be due to immune activation and genetic predisposition, often requiring lifelong hormone replacement.
keyPoints: ~10% incidence of autoimmune endocrine diseases with cancer immunotherapy.
Common conditions: hypophysitis, thyroid disorders, adrenal insufficiency, hypoparathyroidism, diabetes insipidus.
Mechanism involves immune activation and genetic predisposition.
Endocrinopathies are generally irreversible, necessitating lifelong hormone replacement.

items.subtopic: ERYTHROCYTOSIS | Erythrocytosis
items.title: Ectopic Erythropoietin Production and Its Clinical Implications in Paraneoplastic Erythrocytosis
items.content: Paraneoplastic erythrocytosis is primarily caused by ectopic production of erythropoietin by cancer cells, leading to increased red blood cell production. This condition is often asymptomatic but can be detected through elevated hematocrit levels (>52% in men, >48% in women). It is associated with specific cancers such as renal cell carcinoma, hepatoma, and cerebellar hemangioblastomas.
keyPoints: Ectopic erythropoietin production by cancer cells causes most cases of paraneoplastic erythrocytosis.
Hematocrit levels are elevated (>52% in men, >48% in women) in patients with this condition.
Approximately 3% of renal cell carcinoma, 10% of hepatoma, and 15% of cerebellar hemangioblastomas cases present with erythrocytosis.
Measurement of red cell mass is recommended if a patient has cancer associated with erythrocytosis.
Elevated serum erythropoietin levels without other explanations confirm the paraneoplastic syndrome.
Successful resection or effective treatment of the underlying cancer typically resolves erythrocytosis.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · ERYTHROCYTOSIS | Erythrocytosis | GRANULOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: GRANULOCYTOSIS
items.title: Understanding Paraneoplastic Granulocytosis in Cancer Patients
items.content: Granulocytosis, characterized by a granulocyte count >8000/µL, occurs in about 30% of patients with solid tumors. While half of these cases have nonparaneoplastic causes like infection or glucocorticoid administration, the other half is linked to tumor-produced factors such as G-CSF, GM-CSF, and IL-6. Granulocytosis is more common in advanced-stage diseases and does not typically require treatment.
keyPoints: Granulocytosis is defined by a granulocyte count >8000/µL.
Approximately 30% of solid tumor patients exhibit granulocytosis, with half having identifiable nonparaneoplastic causes.
Tumors from lung, ovarian, and bladder cancers can produce G-CSF, GM-CSF, or IL-6, contributing to paraneoplastic granulocytosis.
Granulocytosis is more prevalent in advanced-stage diseases (40% with lung/gastrointestinal cancers, 30% with brain/ovarian cancers).
Paraneoplastic granulocytosis typically resolves upon treatment of the underlying cancer and does not require separate intervention.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · ERYTHROCYTOSIS | Erythrocytosis | GRANULOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: THROMBOCYTOSIS | EOSINOPHILIA | Eosinophilia
items.title: Thrombocytosis as a Marker of Advanced Cancer and Poor Prognosis
items.content: In patients with cancer, thrombocytosis (platelet count >400,000/μL) is often associated with advanced disease stages and poorer prognosis. It occurs in 35% of such cases, frequently linked to IL-6 elevation which may also promote tumor growth directly, especially in ovarian cancer.
keyPoints: Thrombocytosis prevalence: ~35% in patients with cancer
Platelet count threshold for thrombocytosis: >400,000/μL
Cancers associated: 40% lung and gastrointestinal; 20% breast, endometrial, ovarian; 10% lymphoma
IL-6 role: Stimulates platelet production and may promote tumor growth in ovarian cancer
Prognosis: More likely advanced-stage disease with poorer outcomes
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · THROMBOCYTOSIS | EOSINOPHILIA | Eosinophilia

items.subtopic: THROMBOCYTOSIS | EOSINOPHILIA | Eosinophilia
items.title: Eosinophilia Management and Clinical Implications in Cancer Patients
items.content: Eosinophilia, present in ~1% of cancer patients, often requires management focused on the underlying malignancy. It can lead to respiratory symptoms if eosinophil counts exceed 5000/μL, with glucocorticoids being effective for symptom relief.
keyPoints: Eosinophilia prevalence: ~1% in cancer patients
Cancers associated: 10% lymphoma; 3% lung cancer; occasional cervical, gastrointestinal, renal, breast cancers
Symptoms at >5000/μL eosinophils: Shortness of breath, wheezing, pulmonary infiltrates
Treatment focus: Underlying malignancy (surgery/radiation/chemotherapy)
Symptom management: Oral/inhaled glucocorticoids for respiratory symptoms
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · THROMBOCYTOSIS | EOSINOPHILIA | Eosinophilia

items.subtopic: THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Cancer and Thrombosis Risk Factors
items.content: Patients with cancer have a heightened risk for thromboembolic events due to factors such as immobility, tumor-related blood flow obstruction, and the release of procoagulants from tumors. Additionally, certain chemotherapeutic agents increase this risk.
keyPoints: Nearly 15% of patients who develop deep venous thrombosis or pulmonary embolism have a diagnosis of cancer.
Cancer patients are twice as likely to experience postoperative deep venous thrombosis compared to non-cancer patients.
The annual risk of venous thrombosis in cancer patients receiving chemotherapy is about 11%, sixfold higher than the general population.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Evaluation of Thromboembolic Events in Cancer Patients
items.content: The diagnosis of deep venous thrombosis in cancer patients is primarily conducted using impedance plethysmography or bilateral compression ultrasonography. For pulmonary embolism, a combination of chest radiograph, electrocardiogram, arterial blood gas analysis, and ventilation-perfusion scan are used.
keyPoints: Compression ultrasonography reveals noncompressible venous segments indicative of deep venous thrombosis.
Elevated D-dimer levels are less predictive in cancer patients compared to those without cancer.
Patients with mismatched segmental perfusion defects on ventilation-perfusion scans likely have a pulmonary embolus.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.title: Indications for Cancer Screening in Thrombosis Patients
items.content: While routine cancer screening is not recommended for patients with an initial thromboembolic event, further investigation is warranted if the thrombosis is refractory to treatment, occurs at unusual sites, or is recurrent.
keyPoints: 5% of patients without a history of cancer who develop deep venous thrombosis or pulmonary embolism will be diagnosed with cancer within 1 year.
Cancers most commonly associated with thromboembolic episodes include lung, pancreatic, gastrointestinal, breast, ovarian, and genitourinary cancers; lymphomas; and brain tumors.
Further investigation for underlying cancer is indicated if thrombophlebitis or pulmonary embolism is refractory to treatment, occurs in unusual sites, or is migratory or recurrent.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: Thrombophlebitis and Deep Venous Thrombosis | MISCELLANEOUS REMOTE EFFECTS OF CANCER
items.title: Anticoagulation Management in Cancer Patients with DVT/PE
items.content: Patients diagnosed with deep venous thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary embolism (PE) and cancer should initially receive intravenous unfractionated heparin or low-molecular-weight heparin for at least 5 days. Warfarin therapy should commence within 1-2 days, aiming for an INR of 2–3. For patients with proximal DVT who have contraindications to heparin, consider an inferior vena cava filter. Long-term anticoagulation typically lasts 3–6 months with warfarin or 6 months with low-molecular-weight heparin. New oral anticoagulants like apixaban are effective alternatives due to their lack of need for monitoring and dietary independence.
keyPoints: Initial treatment with IV unfractionated heparin or low-molecular-weight heparin for at least 5 days
Warfarin should be started within 1-2 days, targeting an INR of 2–3
Consider inferior vena cava filter in patients with contraindications to heparin
Long-term anticoagulation: Warfarin for 3–6 months or low-molecular-weight heparin for 6 months
Apixaban (10 mg bid for 7 days, then 5 mg bid for 6 months) is noninferior to dalteparin in cancer patients with DVT/PE
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Thrombophlebitis and Deep Venous Thrombosis | MISCELLANEOUS REMOTE EFFECTS OF CANCER

items.subtopic: Thrombophlebitis and Deep Venous Thrombosis | MISCELLANEOUS REMOTE EFFECTS OF CANCER
items.title: Prophylaxis Strategies for Cancer Patients at Risk of Thromboembolism
items.content: Cancer patients, especially those undergoing major surgery or chemotherapy, should be evaluated for thromboembolic prophylaxis. Prophylactic measures include heparin and pneumatic boots for surgical cases, and low-molecular-weight heparin or aspirin for those on thalidomide analogues. The Khorana risk score helps assess the likelihood of thrombosis based on cancer type, therapy, blood counts, and BMI. Studies indicate a 50% reduction in thrombotic events with rivaroxaban or apixaban prophylaxis in high-risk patients, though bleeding risks are about 5%. Routine prophylaxis during chemotherapy remains controversial.
keyPoints: Evaluate cancer patients for thromboembolic prophylaxis, especially post-surgery and during chemotherapy
Use heparin or pneumatic boots for surgical cases; low-molecular-weight heparin or aspirin for thalidomide analogues
Khorana risk score assesses thrombosis likelihood based on cancer type, therapy, blood counts, BMI
Rivaroxaban and apixaban reduce thrombotic events by 50% in high-risk patients with a bleeding risk of about 5%

items.statement: Autoimmune endocrine diseases from cancer immunotherapy are reversible with treatment.
items.explanation: These autoimmune endocrinopathies are generally irreversible and require lifelong hormone replacement.

items.statement: Most patients with erythrocytosis due to cancer are symptomatic.
items.explanation: In most cases, the erythrocytosis is asymptomatic.

items.subtopic: ERYTHROCYTOSIS | Erythrocytosis | GRANULOCYTOSIS
items.statement: Granulocytosis in cancer patients often requires treatment.
items.explanation: Paraneoplastic granulocytosis does not require treatment and resolves with the treatment of the underlying cancer.

items.statement: Patients with thrombocytosis are more likely to have advanced-stage disease and a poorer prognosis.
items.explanation: Thrombocytosis is associated with advanced-stage disease and poorer prognosis.

items.subtopic: THROMBOCYTOSIS | EOSINOPHILIA | Eosinophilia
items.statement: Eosinophilia in cancer patients is typically symptomatic.
items.explanation: Patients with eosinophilia are typically asymptomatic.

items.subtopic: THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Elevation of D-dimer is as predictive of deep venous thrombosis in patients with cancer as it is in patients without cancer.
items.explanation: Elevation of D-dimer is not as predictive of deep venous thrombosis in patients with cancer as it is in those without cancer.

items.subtopic: THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.statement: Patients with symptoms and signs suggesting a pulmonary embolism should be evaluated with a ventilation-perfusion scan.
items.explanation: Patients with symptoms and signs of pulmonary embolism are evaluated using a ventilation-perfusion scan.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.statement: Prophylaxis with low-molecular-weight heparin or low-dose aspirin is recommended for all hospitalized cancer patients.
items.explanation: Guidelines recommend prophylaxis specifically for hospitalized cancer patients and those receiving thalidomide analogues, not all hospitalized cancer patients.

items.statement: The Khorana risk score takes into account the type of cancer, therapy, blood counts, and body mass index.
items.explanation: The Khorana risk score considers these factors to assess thrombosis risk in cancer patients.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Thrombophlebitis and Deep Venous Thrombosis | MISCELLANEOUS REMOTE EFFECTS OF CANCER

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Syndromes: Endocrinologic/Hematologic | ENDOCRINE PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES
items.assertion: Ectopic hormone production is often a quantitative change rather than an absolute change in tissue expression.
items.reason: Many hormones are produced at low levels from tissues other than the classic endocrine source.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason both explain why ectopic hormone production is considered a quantitative change.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Paraneoplastic Syndromes: Endocrinologic/Hematologic | ENDOCRINE PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Paraneoplastic Syndromes: Endocrinologic/Hematologic | ENDOCRINE PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES
items.assertion: Ectopic ACTH production can lead to Cushing's syndrome.
items.reason: Cushing's syndrome is caused by excessive and unregulated production of vasopressin.
items.explanation: The reason is incorrect; Cushing's syndrome results from ectopic ACTH, not vasopressin.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Paraneoplastic Syndromes: Endocrinologic/Hematologic | ENDOCRINE PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy | ECTOPIC VASOPRESSIN: TUMOR-ASSOCIATED SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE
items.assertion: Hyponatremia in SIADH is due to excessive vasopressin production.
items.reason: Excessive vasopressin leads to water retention and dilutional hyponatremia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy | ECTOPIC VASOPRESSIN: TUMOR-ASSOCIATED SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE

items.subtopic: Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy | ECTOPIC VASOPRESSIN: TUMOR-ASSOCIATED SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE
items.assertion: Denosumab is used as a first-line treatment for HHM.
items.reason: Denosumab acts by inhibiting osteoclast activity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; denosumab is not typically the first-line treatment for HHM.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy | ECTOPIC VASOPRESSIN: TUMOR-ASSOCIATED SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE

items.subtopic: Ectopic Vasopressin: Tumor-Associated SIADH
items.assertion: Fluid restriction alone can often partially correct hyponatremia in patients with ectopic vasopressin production.
items.reason: Strict monitoring of the amount and types of liquids consumed or administered intravenously is required for fluid restriction to be effective.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why fluid restriction can help.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Ectopic Vasopressin: Tumor-Associated SIADH

items.subtopic: CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).
items.assertion: Ectopic ACTH production can lead to severe hypokalemia.
items.reason: High cortisol levels overwhelm the renal $11\beta$-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type II enzyme, leading to mineralocorticoid receptor activation by cortisol.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).

items.subtopic: CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).
items.assertion: High-dose dexamethasone always suppresses ectopic ACTH production.
items.reason: Ectopic sources of ACTH do not respond to glucocorticoid suppression in most cases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · CUSHING'S SYNDROME CAUSED BY ECTOPIC ACTH PRODUCTION (See also Chap. 398).

items.subtopic: Cushing's Syndrome Caused by Ectopic ACTH Production | TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II
items.assertion: IGF-II suppresses growth hormone and insulin.
items.reason: This suppression results in reduced IGF binding protein 3, IGF-I, and acid-labile subunit.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why IGF-II suppresses growth hormone and insulin.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Cushing's Syndrome Caused by Ectopic ACTH Production | TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II

items.subtopic: Cushing's Syndrome Caused by Ectopic ACTH Production | TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II
items.assertion: IGF-II assays always detect IGF-II precursors.
items.reason: Serum IGF-II levels may not be increased despite causing hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because IGF-II assays may not detect IGF-II precursors, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · Cushing's Syndrome Caused by Ectopic ACTH Production | TUMOR-INDUCED HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSED BY EXCESS PRODUCTION OF INSULIN-LIKE GROWTH FACTOR TYPE II

items.assertion: Autoimmune endocrine diseases can emerge early or several months after starting immunotherapy.
items.reason: Checkpoint inhibitor-induced immune activation exacerbates underlying genetic predisposition and autoimmunity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why autoimmune endocrine diseases may present at different times post-immunotherapy.

items.assertion: Paraneoplastic thrombocytosis does not require treatment other than the underlying tumor.
items.reason: Thrombocytosis is not clearly linked to thrombosis in patients with cancer.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why paraneoplastic thrombocytosis doesn't require separate treatment.

items.subtopic: THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.assertion: Migratory or recurrent thrombophlebitis may be the initial manifestation of cancer.
items.reason: Trousseau's syndrome is characterized by migratory thrombophlebitis and visceral carcinoma, particularly pancreatic cancer.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS
items.assertion: Patients with cancer who undergo surgical procedures requiring general anesthesia have a 20-30% risk of deep venous thrombosis.
items.reason: Cancer patients are predisposed to thromboembolism due to immobilization and tumor-related blood flow obstruction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the direct explanation for the increased risk after surgery.
items.reference: Paraneoplastic Syndromes Endocrinologic Hematologic · THROMBOPHLEBITIS AND DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS

items.subtopic: Thrombophlebitis and Deep Venous Thrombosis | MISCELLANEOUS REMOTE EFFECTS OF CANCER
items.assertion: Prophylaxis with oral anticoagulants like apixaban is noninferior to dalteparin in cancer patients with deep vein thrombosis.
items.reason: Oral anticoagulants do not require close monitoring of the prothrombin time and are unaffected by dietary factors.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason given does not explain why apixaban is noninferior to dalteparin.
